purpose: Determines whether yield curve steepening reflects improving growth expectations (good, front end falling) or deteriorating fiscal/supply dynamics (bad, back end rising), with qualitatively different implications for asset allocation
components: Term premium decomposition via ACM/Kim-Wright models
Breakeven inflation behavior (rising = growth-driven, flat = supply-driven)
MOVE index regime classification (<80/80-120/>120)
Real yield vs nominal yield decomposition
Equity market response (rallying = good, falling = bad)
current_assessment: Current steepening is predominantly bad (term-premium-driven). ACM term premium +50 to +100bp, breakevens range-bound at 2.2-2.5%, 2023 Q3 provides clean template. Confidence 8/10 with 1-point deduction each for model dependence and partial circularity.
limitations: Model-dependent: relies on term premium estimates with 30-50bp irreducible uncertainty
Good/bad classification is itself partially model-dependent
Historical precedents for bad steepening are limited (2013, 2018 Q4, 2023 Q3)
Current MOVE in ambiguous middle zone (~95-110) limits diagnostic value
Cannot distinguish transient from structural bad steepening

purpose: Provides a properly calibrated assessment of the yield curve's recession-predictive value, replacing the misleading '7/7' framing with Bayesian posterior probabilities
components: Base rate of recession in any 24-month window (25-30%)
Prior yield curve inversions as evidence (including false positives: 1966, 1998, 2022-23)
Variable lead time distribution (6-24 months)
Term premium conditioning to separate expectations-driven from contaminated signals
MOVE-based signal quality diagnostic
current_assessment: Marginal predictive lift is approximately 1.5-2x base rate after proper Bayesian adjustment. The 2022-23 deep inversion (-108bp) without recession (as of March 2026) has either broken the record (7/8) or implies an operationally useless 3+ year lead time. Signal retains value but is far from near-certainty.
limitations: Small sample size constrains all threshold estimates
MOVE conditioning thresholds derived from ~5 observations, likely overfitted
Cannot distinguish between 'false positive' and 'very long lead time'
Term premium conditioning adds model uncertainty
Does not incorporate global curve synchronization

purpose: Maps the nonlinear transmission mechanism from yield curve/SOFR dynamics to real credit supply through the CLO arbitrage viability threshold
components: SOFR level relative to CLO equity tranche hurdle rate
CLO AAA spreads and liability costs
Reinvestment period status (active vs expired)
OC test headroom and CCC bucket concentration
PIK toggle usage as early stress indicator
Private credit substitution and NAV smoothing dynamics
current_assessment: SOFR at ~4.3-4.5% places CLO arbitrage in a marginal-viability zone. The channel is the most mechanically direct link from rates to credit supply, with 50bp moves producing binary outcomes. $250-350B in reinvestment expirations create asymmetric stress exposure. Private credit ($1.7T) masks aggregate stress signals with 12-18 month lag.
limitations: Single-agent development (credit_analyst_02)
Binary threshold precise location depends on individual CLO vintage and structure
Interaction with equity discount rate channel not modeled
PIK toggle data may lag actual stress
Private credit signal degradation untested through full cycle at current scale

purpose: Evaluates whether the US is approaching the self-reinforcing fiscal spiral where rising term premium → higher interest costs → more issuance → higher term premium
components: Net interest expense relative to nominal GDP growth (r-g gap)
Average maturity and repricing speed (~16%/year at 6.2yr avg maturity)
Central bank intervention likelihood (QE return probability)
Reserve currency premium buffer (potentially 200bp+)
Geopolitical fiscal ratchet contribution
International demand dynamics (BOJ normalization, sanctions architecture)
current_assessment: Mechanism validated by UK gilt crisis for developed-market sovereigns. US net interest approaching $1T, blended rate ~4.5% approaching nominal GDP ~5.5%. But activation threshold is genuinely unknown: reserve currency premium could provide 200bp+ buffer; blended rate moves slowly (years, not quarters); and the loop requires multiple conditions to simultaneously hold (conjunction fallacy risk). Range of informed opinion spans 'quarters' to 'decades.'
limitations: US-specific threshold genuinely unknown
Reserve currency premium is real but unquantifiable
Conjunction fallacy: requires multiple conditions to simultaneously hold
Japan counterexample shows central bank can override for decades
Blended rate moves slowly enough that the timeline is multi-year at minimum

purpose: Translates the maturity-dependent correlation bifurcation into actionable portfolio construction: which maturities hedge equities vs which add correlated risk
components: 2-5Y as equity hedge (negative stock-bond correlation, cyclically responsive)
10-30Y as separate correlated risk factor (positive correlation, fiscal supply dynamics)
Term premium level as regime indicator (transitional zone at 50-100bp)
MOVE regime for bifurcation stability assessment
RSP vs SPY as trade expression for term premium shock positioning
current_assessment: Bifurcation is currently operative and has been confirmed across 6 iterations by 8 agents. Term premium at 50-100bp is in the inherently unstable transitional zone with $15-20T+ in strategies calibrated to the prior negative-correlation regime. Only 3 historical transitions exist with different macro contexts.
limitations: Only 3 historical correlation regime transitions to calibrate from
Transitional zone (50-100bp term premium) is inherently unstable
Could resolve via policy intervention within 3-14 months per KB precedents
Equal-weighted vs cap-weighted ERP gap (200bp) complicates index-level positioning
Factor rotation prescriptions carry false precision at sector level

purpose: Establishes the bounds of what can and cannot be known about the term premium in real-time, and how to propagate uncertainty through downstream analysis
components: ACM model estimate
Kim-Wright model estimate
Model divergence range (30-50bp)
MOVE-based heuristic cross-check
TIPS breakeven cross-check
Survey-based expectations as anchor
Basis trade distortion adjustment (10-60bp range)
current_assessment: Term premium is estimated at 50-130bp depending on model, with 30-50bp irreducible model divergence. Any claim built on a specific point estimate carries at minimum ±30-50bp uncertainty. The basis trade further distorts readings by 10-60bp. Operationally, analysis should use ranges rather than point estimates and propagate uncertainty to all downstream claims.
limitations: No model-free estimator currently exists
30-50bp divergence on 50-100bp estimate creates signal-to-noise approaching 1:1
Basis trade distortion (10-60bp) adds a second layer of measurement uncertainty
All downstream quantitative claims inherit this uncertainty
Real-time estimates subject to significant revision
